perturbation.original_text: This Agreement shall be interpreted in accordance with and governed by the substantive and procedural laws of India and the parties hereby consent to the jurisdiction of Courts and/or Forums situated at <Name of the city> .
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement shall be interpreted in accordance with the laws of <Arbitrary Country> and the parties hereby consent to the jurisdiction of Courts and/or Forums situated at <Name of the city>.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the governing law to a country other than India potentially invalidates consumer protections afforded under Indian law, particularly regarding data privacy and contractual fairness. The choice of law is no longer compliant with Indian jurisdiction.
perturbation.law_citation: Section 43A of the Information Technology Act, 2000
perturbation.law_explanation: Section 43A of the Information Technology Act, 2000, mandates reasonable security practices for sensitive personal data. By changing the governing law to a foreign country, the contract attempts to bypass the data protection requirements of the IT Act, which applies to entities processing data in India, thus contradicting the law.

perturbation.justification: The original contract stipulates that it is governed by the laws of India, implicitly ensuring consumer protections afforded under Indian law. The changed text replaces Indian law with the laws of an arbitrary country, removing the legal certainty that Indian laws apply. While the scraped snippets are unhelpful, the Law Explanation states that Section 43A of the Information Technology Act, 2000, mandates reasonable security practices for sensitive personal data. By changing the governing law to a foreign country, the contract attempts to bypass the data protection requirements of the IT Act, contradicting the law. This constitutes an omission of legally mandated consumer protection, which aligns with the definition of 'Omission Legal Contradiction'.

perturbation.original_text: In the event of dispute aris ing between the parties in connection with the validity, interpretation, and implementation or alleged breach of any provision of this Agreement, the parties shall attempt to resolve the dispute in good faith by senior level negotiations. In case, any such difference or dispute is not amicably resolved within forty five (45) days of such referra l for negotiations, it shall be resolved through arbitration process, wherein both the parties will appoint one arbitrator each and the third one will be appointed by the two arbitrators in accordance with the Arbitration and Conciliation Act, 1996.
perturbation.changed_text: In the event of dispute aris ing between the parties in connection with the validity, interpretation, and implementation or alleged breach of any provision of this Agreement, the parties may attempt to resolve the dispute. Any further action shall be at the sole discretion of the Auditee.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the mandatory arbitration clause and granting sole discretion to the Auditee contradicts the principles of fair dispute resolution and the Arbitration and Conciliation Act, 1996, especially concerning disputes involving data protection and privacy under the IT Act, as it creates an unfair advantage for one party.
perturbation.law_explanation: Section 8 of the Arbitration and Conciliation Act, 1996, mandates that judicial authorities refer parties to arbitration if there is an arbitration agreement. By removing the arbitration clause and granting sole discretion to one party, the contract prevents the mandatory referral to arbitration, thus contradicting the Act and hindering fair dispute resolution.

perturbation.justification: The original text mandated arbitration, aligning with Section 8 of the Arbitration and Conciliation Act, 1996, as highlighted in the Law Explanation. The changed text eliminates this mandatory arbitration and grants sole discretion to the Auditee, thus preventing mandatory referral to arbitration and contradicting the principle of fair dispute resolution. While the scraped law snippets are not available to directly support this, the removal of a legally required consumer protection, as defined in the Omission Legal Contradiction definition, exists. The Perturbation Explanation confirms that this removal creates an unfair advantage, violating the Arbitration and Conciliation Act, 1996. The deletion of the arbitration clause, and granting complete autonomy to the Auditee, clearly demonstrates a non-compliance, because it directly negates the obligation to resolve disputes through arbitration.

perturbation.original_text: Auditor acknowledges that any actual or threatened disclosure or use of the Confidential Information by Aud itor would be a breach of this agreement and may cause immediate and irreparable harm to Auditee or to its clients; Auditor affirms that damages from such disclosure or use by it may be impossible to measure accurately; and injury sustained by Auditee / it s clients may be impossible to calculate and compensate fully. Therefore, Auditor acknowledges that in the event of such a breach, Auditee shall be entitled to specific performance by Auditor of its obligations contained in this Agreement. In addition , Auditor shall compensate the Auditee for the loss or damages caused to the auditee , actual and liquidated damages , which may be demanded by Auditee with l iquidated damages not to exceed the Contract value.
perturbation.changed_text: Auditor acknowledges that any actual or threatened disclosure or use of the Confidential Information by Auditor would be a breach of this agreement. In the event of such a breach, damages shall be limited to a nominal amount of INR 1000, and Auditee waives any right to seek specific performance or injunctive relief.
perturbation.explanation: Limiting damages to a nominal amount and waiving the right to specific performance or injunctive relief severely restricts the Auditee's remedies for a breach of confidentiality, potentially conflicting with the principles of adequate compensation under Indian contract law, particularly considering the severe potential damage from data breaches.
perturbation.law_citation: Section 73 of the Indian Contract Act, 1872
perturbation.law_explanation: Section 73 of the Indian Contract Act, 1872, provides for compensation for loss or damage caused by a breach of contract. By limiting damages to a nominal amount, the contract undermines the principle of reasonable compensation for actual losses incurred due to a breach of confidentiality, potentially invalidating this clause under Indian law.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Compensation of loss or damage caused by breach of contract | Indian Contract Act, 1872 | Bare Acts | Law Library | AdvocateKhoj Follow @SCJudgments Login : Advocate | Client Indian Contract Act, 1872 73. Compensation of loss or damage caused by breach of contract When a contract has been broken, the party who suffers by such breach is entitled to receive, from the party who has broken the contract, compensation for any loss or damage caused to him thereby, which naturally arose in the usual course of things from such breach, or which the parties knew, when they made the contract, to be likely to result from the breach of it. Such compensation is not to be given for any remote and indirect loss or damage sustained by reason of the breach. Compensation for failure to discharge obligation resembling those created by contract: When an obligation resembling those created by contract has been incurred and has not been discharged, any person injured by the failure to discharge it is entitled to receive the same compensation from the party in default, as if such person had contracted to discharge it and had broken his contract. Explanation: In estimating the loss or damage arising from a breach of contract, the means which existed of remedying the inconvenience caused by non-performance of the contract must be taken into account. Illustrations (a) A contracts to sell and deliver 50 maunds of saltpetre to B, at a certain price to be paid on delivery. A breaks his promise. B is entitled to receive from A, by way of compensation, the sum, if any, by which the contract price falls short of the price for which B might have obtained 50 maunds of saltpetre of like quality at the time when the saltpetre ought to have been delivered. (b) A hires B's ship to go to Bombay, and there takes on board, on the first of January, a cargo, which A is to provide, and to bring it to Calcutta, the freight to be paid when earned. B's ship does not go to Bombay, but A has opportunities for procuring suitable conveyance for the cargo upon terms as advantageous as those on which he h
perturbation.justification: The changed text directly contradicts Section 73 of the Indian Contract Act, 1872, which mandates compensation for loss or damage caused by a breach of contract. The modified text limits damages to a nominal INR 1000 and waives specific performance, negating the right to 'receive, from the party who has broken the contract, compensation for any loss or damage caused to him thereby' as stated in the scraped law snippet. The original text provided for specific performance and actual/liquidated damages, but this was removed.

perturbation.original_text: Both parties agree that all of their obligations undertaken herein with respect to Confidential Information received pursuant to this Agreement shall survive till perpetui ty even after expiration or termination of this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: Both parties agree that all of their obligations undertaken herein with respect to Confidential Information received pursuant to this Agreement shall expire immediately after termination of this Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the obligation for confidential information to survive only until termination directly contradicts standard practices and legal expectations around NDAs, which often require confidentiality to be maintained for several years, or perpetually, especially considering data protection regulations. An immediate expiration of confidentiality upon termination creates a loophole violating general obligations around data privacy.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Section 72 of The Information Technology Act, 2000
perturbation.law_citation: Section 72 of The Information Technology Act, 2000
perturbation.law_explanation: Section 72 of The Information Technology Act, 2000 addresses breach of confidentiality and privacy. This clause limits the responsibilities of maintaining data privacy, where any data breach or sharing of private information is not legally bound to the auditor after the termination of the contract. That may contradict with Section 72 because the auditor would be relieved of their responsibility, even if they had access to information during the contract.

perturbation.justification: The original text ensures that confidentiality obligations survive even after the agreement terminates. The changed text stipulates that these obligations expire immediately upon termination. The perturbation explanation states that this creates a loophole violating data privacy obligations. The Law Explanation states that section 72 of The Information Technology Act, 2000, addresses breach of confidentiality and privacy, suggesting that limiting the responsibilities of maintaining data privacy could contradict the Act. Scraped Law Snippet 1 concerns the Right of Children to Free and Compulsory Education Act, 2009, which is irrelevant in this context as it does not address data privacy or confidentiality obligations, but the other elements are sufficient to make a determination. This directly contradicts legal expectations around data privacy, and falls under Omission Legal Contradiction by creating a loophole.
